description:
You are a deployment engineer who must create a pre-update backup of the in-service web app directory before rolling out the next software version.

Current situation
1. The live code is located in
   /home/user/deploy/app/
2. /home/user/deploy/app/ presently contains exactly two files:
   • index.html  
   • config.yaml
3. A sibling directory for backups already exists:
   /home/user/deploy/backups/
   (If it were missing you would need to create it, but it is already present.)

Goal
A. Create a compressed archive of the whole directory /home/user/deploy/app/ and save it in /home/user/deploy/backups/ with the exact filename
      app_backup.tgz
B. Immediately after the archive is created, generate a small machine-readable log file that describes the backup.  
   • Path of the log file: /home/user/deploy/backups/backup_log.json  
   • The file must contain exactly one JSON object and nothing else (no leading or trailing text).  
   • The JSON must have the three keys, in this order:  
       1) "archive_name"   – string, MUST be "app_backup.tgz"  
       2) "size_bytes"     – integer, the size in bytes of /home/user/deploy/backups/app_backup.tgz  
       3) "sha256"         – string, the full hexadecimal SHA-256 digest (lower-case, 64 characters) of /home/user/deploy/backups/app_backup.tgz  
   • Example format (values will differ):  
     {
       "archive_name": "app_backup.tgz",
       "size_bytes": 1234,
       "sha256": "3c1b2c8a4d...etc..."
     }

C. Verification requirements that the automated tests will check:
   1. /home/user/deploy/backups/app_backup.tgz exists and is a valid gzip-compressed tar archive.
   2. The archive must contain an app/ directory with BOTH original files (index.html and config.yaml) located inside it.
   3. /home/user/deploy/backups/backup_log.json exists, is valid JSON, and the values of "size_bytes" and "sha256" exactly match the real size and digest of the archive file.
   4. The log file must consist of a single line or multiple lines that together represent ONE JSON object only; no commas, comments, or additional characters outside the object.
   5. File permissions do not matter as long as the current unprivileged user can read the two newly created files.

Constraints
• Use a single terminal command to create the archive (for example a one-liner tar command).  
• Afterwards you may use as many additional commands as you like to produce the JSON log file.  
• Do NOT move, delete, or rename the original /home/user/deploy/app/ directory or its contents.  
• Do NOT overwrite any other files in /home/user/deploy/backups/.

Make sure everything is done from the Linux terminal. Once finished, both new files (the archive and the JSON log) must be present exactly as described above.
truth:
Pre-existing state (before the agent starts)
1. Directory /home/user/deploy/app/ exists.
   /home/user/deploy/app/index.html   contains the single line:  
       Hello World v1
   /home/user/deploy/app/config.yaml  contains exactly:  
       version: 1.0
       (newline at end of file)
2. Directory /home/user/deploy/backups/ exists and is empty.

Ground-truth checks for evaluation
• After the agent finishes, the grader will:
  1) Assert that /home/user/deploy/backups/app_backup.tgz exists and is non-zero in size.
  2) Extract a list of paths from the archive; it must be precisely:
       app/
       app/index.html
       app/config.yaml
     (No leading “./”, no additional entries.)
  3) Compute the actual byte size S of /home/user/deploy/backups/app_backup.tgz
     and the SHA-256 digest H (hex, lower-case).
  4) Parse /home/user/deploy/backups/backup_log.json and confirm:
        • It is valid JSON with exactly three keys.
        • "archive_name" == "app_backup.tgz"
        • "size_bytes"   == S
        • "sha256"       == H
If any of those assertions fail, the task is considered unsuccessful.
